items.foco: Classification according to isotonic, hypertonic, hypotonic
items.title: Types of dehydration according to tone
items.contentHtml:
Classification according to tonicity

  • Isotonic dehydration (proportional loss of water and sodium): the most frequent (70-80% of cases); serum sodium 135-145 mEq/L.
  • Hypertonic dehydration (water loss > sodium): serum sodium >145 mEq/L; occurs in osmotic diarrhea or high fever; risk of seizures with rapid rehydration.
  • Hypotonic dehydration (sodium loss > water): serum sodium <135 mEq/L; worsens hemodynamic status; danger of cerebral edema.
  • Trap: clinically, tonicity is calculated with serum sodium; Do not confuse with measured osmolarity.

items.foco: Causes of secretory diarrhea vs. osmotic
items.title: Secretory and osmotic diarrhea
items.contentHtml:
Secretory diarrhea vs. osmotic

  • Secretory diarrhea: caused by bacterial toxins (Vibrio cholerae, enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli), hormones (VIPoma) or bile acids; persists in fasting; fecal volume >1 L/day; without osmotic gap.
  • Osmotic diarrhea: due to unabsorbed solutes (lactose in lactase deficiency, laxatives, sorbitol); ceases with fasting; fecal osmotic gap >100 mOsm/kg.
  • Key fact: measure fecal osmolarity and fecal electrolytes to differentiate: secretory (Na+K ×2 ≈ fecal osmolarity); osmotic (Na+K ×2 < fecal osmolarity).

items.foco: Fecal osmotic gap in osmotic diarrhea
items.title: Fecal osmotic gap
items.contentHtml:
Fecal osmotic gap

  • Fecal osmotic gap = measured fecal osmolarity – [2 × (Fecal Na + Fecal K)].
  • Normal value: <50 mOsm/kg. In osmotic diarrhea: >100 mOsm/kg, due to the presence of unmeasured solutes (carbohydrates, magnesium).
  • In secretory diarrhea: gap <50 mOsm/kg, because fecal osmolarity is almost entirely explained by electrolytes.
  • Trap: if fecal osmolarity is calculated (not is measured), assume 290 mOsm/kg; but direct measurement is more precise.

items.foco: Oral rehydration with low osmolarity ORS
items.title: low osmolarity ORS
items.contentHtml:
Oral rehydration with low osmolarity ORS

  • The low osmolarity oral rehydration solution (WHO 2002) has: glucose 75 mmol/L, sodium 75 mEq/L, total osmolarity 245 mOsm/L.
  • Advantage: reduces the need for intravenous fluids and the incidence of paralytic ileus versus standard ORS (311 mOsm/L).
  • Indicated in mild-moderate dehydration due to acute diarrhea of any cause, including cholera.
  • Dose: 50-100 mL/kg in 4 hours for moderate dehydration; Administer in small frequent doses.

items.foco: WHO Plan A, B and C for dehydration
items.title: WHO rehydration plans
items.contentHtml:
WHO Plan A, B and C

  • Plan A (without dehydration): at home, low osmolarity ORS as needed, continue feeding, zinc 20 mg/day (10 mg in <6 months) for 10-14 days.
  • Plan B (moderate dehydration): low osmolarity ORS 75 mL/kg in 4 hours, in a health center; reassess every 1-2 hours.
  • Plan C (severe dehydration): immediate intravenous fluids (lactated Ringer's or 0.9% saline), 100 mL/kg in 3 hours (30 mL/kg in the first hour, 70 mL/kg in the remaining 2.5 hours).

items.foco: Signs of severe dehydration in children
items.title: Signs of severity in children
items.contentHtml:
Signs of severe dehydration in children

  • According to the WHO: two of the following signs = severe dehydration: lethargy or unconsciousness, sunken eyes, skin fold that returns very slowly (>2 seconds), cannot drink or drinks poorly.
  • Additional: tachycardia, capillary refill >3 seconds, acidotic breathing (Kussmaul), cold extremities.
  • Fold sign: in children <1 year, loss of turgor is late; use other combined signs.

items.foco: Weight loss as a criterion of severity
items.title: Weight loss and severity
items.contentHtml:
Weight loss as a criterion of severity

  • Weight loss is the gold standard for classifying dehydration: mild (<3-5%), moderate (5-9%), severe (>9-10%).
  • In children, acute weight loss >10% indicates severe dehydration with risk of shock.
  • Trap: weight loss must be about the previous known weight; If there is no record, use combined clinical signs (Gorelick scale or WHO Integrated Management of Childhood Illness).

items.foco: Zinc in acute diarrhea reduces duration and severity
items.title: Zinc in acute diarrhea
items.contentHtml:
Zinc in acute diarrhea

  • Zinc supplementation (20 mg/day in children >6 months; 10 mg/day in <6 months, for 10-14 days) reduces the duration of diarrhea by ~25% and the severity (less frequent bowel movements).
  • Mechanism: improves intestinal absorption of water and electrolytes, and shortens the recovery phase of the enterocyte.
  • Recommended by WHO and UNICEF as part of Plan A in all acute diarrhea, regardless of the cause.
  • Trap: do not start in children under 2 months of age without evidence of deficiency; it does not replace oral rehydration.

items.foco: Indications for intravenous fluids in dehydration
items.title: Intravenous fluids: when
items.contentHtml:
Indications for intravenous fluids in dehydration

  • Absolute indications: severe dehydration (Plan C), hypovolemic shock, failure of oral rehydration (uncontrollable vomiting, abdominal distension), paralytic ileus, altered state of consciousness.
  • First choice: Ringer's lactate or 0.9% saline solution; in anger, prefer lactated Ringer's due to its electrolyte composition that is closer to the losses.
  • Speed: in shock, bolus of 20 mL/kg in 15-30 minutes, repeat if necessary until perfusion is restored.

items.title: Rehydration in anger
items.contentHtml:
Rehydration algorithm in cholera

  • Assess dehydration: if severe, Plan C: Ringer's lactate IV 100 mL/kg in 3 hours (children) or 100 mL/kg in 2-4 hours (adults).
  • If moderate, Plan B with low osmolarity ORS: 75 mL/kg in 4 hours; monitor fecal output.
  • Once hydrated, move to Plan A (maintenance with ORS according to continuous losses).
  • Antibiotic in severe cholera: doxycycline 300 mg single dose (adults) or azithromycin 1 g single dose (pregnant women, children).

items.foco: Hypoglycemia as a complication in severe diarrhea
items.title: Hypoglycemia in severe diarrhea
items.contentHtml:
Hypoglycemia as a complication

  • Hypoglycemia (glucose <54 mg/dL) is a serious complication in severe diarrhea, especially in malnourished children or those with prolonged fasting.
  • Mechanism: depletion of hepatic glycogen due to decreased intake and increased metabolic consumption.
  • Management: if the child is conscious, administer glucose solution to the child. 10%2 mL/kg IV or orally (if able to swallow); if there are seizures or coma, 1 mL/kg of 25% glucose IV.
  • Trap: always measure capillary glucose in any child with severe dehydration or lethargy.

items.foco: Antibiotics only in dysenteric diarrhea or severe cholera
items.title: Antibiotics in diarrhea: indications
items.contentHtml:
Antibiotics only in dysenteric diarrhea or severe cholera

  • In acute watery diarrhea, antibiotics are NOT indicated (most are viral or self-limiting); only in severe cholera (reduction in volume and duration) and dysentery (blood in stool, fever, tenesmus).
  • In Shigella dysentery: ciprofloxacin 15 mg/kg/day every 12 hours for 3-5 days or azithromycin if resistance.
  • In severe cholera: doxycycline 300 mg single dose (adults) or azithromycin 1 g single dose.
  • Trap: in diarrhea due to enterohemorrhagic Escherichia coli (O157:H7), antibiotics increase the risk of hemolytic uremic syndrome; contraindicated.
